name: Cacus
description: Cacus was a fire-breathing giant and monstrous son of Vulcan in Roman mythology. He resided in a cave on the Aventine Hill, pre-dating the founding of Rome itself. Cacus is primarily known for his encounter with Hercules after the hero had slain Geryon and was driving his cattle through Italy. Cacus, driven by greed, stole some of Hercules's cattle while the hero slept. To conceal his theft, Cacus dragged the cattle backwards into his cave, making it appear as though they had exited rather than entered. Hercules, upon discovering the theft, searched for the missing cattle. Eventually, he heard the lowing of one of the stolen animals from within Cacus's cave. Enraged, Hercules stormed the cave and slew Cacus, avenging the theft and recovering his cattle. This act of heroism further cemented Hercules's legendary status and contributed to the mythology surrounding the eventual founding of Rome.
subtitle: Fire-breathing giant slain by Hercules
appearance: Cacus is typically depicted as a monstrous giant with the ability to breathe fire. He is often described as having a fearsome appearance, befitting his role as a villainous figure.
habitat: Cacus dwelled in a cave on the Aventine Hill, one of the seven hills of Rome.
behavior: Cacus was known for his thievery, deception, and violent nature. He was motivated by greed and used his strength and cunning to steal from others.
weaknesses: Susceptibility to being tricked by sound

keyMyths.title: The Theft of Hercules' Cattle
keyMyths.description: Cacus, driven by greed, stole some of Hercules's cattle while the hero slept. To conceal his theft, Cacus dragged the cattle backwards into his cave, making it appear as though they had exited rather than entered. This act of deception angered Hercules, leading to their confrontation.

keyMyths.title: Hercules's Revenge
keyMyths.description: Upon discovering the theft, Hercules searched for the missing cattle. He eventually heard the lowing of one of the stolen animals from within Cacus's cave. Enraged, Hercules stormed the cave and slew Cacus, avenging the theft and recovering his cattle. This act demonstrated Hercules's great strength and justice.
keyMyths.source: Livy, Ab Urbe Condita, Book I

keyMyths.title: Cacus's Cave
keyMyths.description: Cacus's cave on the Aventine Hill was described as a dark and forbidding place, reflecting the monstrous nature of its inhabitant. The cave became a symbol of the dangers that Hercules overcame during his labors and travels. It was a formidable lair, difficult to access and defend.

sources.author: Virgil
sources.description: Virgil's Aeneid provides a detailed account of Cacus's encounter with Hercules and the events leading to Cacus's death. It paints a picture of Cacus as a monstrous figure.
sources.text: Virgil's Aeneid provides a detailed account of Cacus's encounter with Hercules and the events leading to Cacus's death. It paints a picture of Cacus as a monstrous figure.

sources.author: Livy
sources.description: Livy's history of Rome recounts the story of Cacus as part of the pre-Roman history of the Aventine Hill. He emphasizes Hercules's role in bringing justice.
sources.text: Livy's history of Rome recounts the story of Cacus as part of the pre-Roman history of the Aventine Hill. He emphasizes Hercules's role in bringing justice.

sources.author: Propertius
sources.description: Propertius mentions Cacus in his elegies, further solidifying Cacus's place in Roman mythology and literature. He focuses on the setting of the cave.
